title: Wingmen

jokes.text: Bret responds to being asked where he's been — with bread — by launching into an elaborate, romantic folk ballad
jokes.setup: Jemaine has been waiting hours for bread; asks Bret where he's been — a mundane domestic question
jokes.punchline: Bret answers not in words but by breaking into a sweeping poetic folk song about a mysterious woman, as if this is a perfectly normal response
jokes.explanation: The comedic engine of the entire opening sequence. The gap between the mundane request (bread) and the grandiose musical response is the joke, and it's perfectly calibrated to the show's DNA. What makes it exceptional is that Bret treats the song as a literal answer — not a detour but the actual reply. The song's overwrought romanticism is funny on its own, but the framing of it as an explanation to a flatmate is what elevates it. Scores 8+ because this joke is the structural premise of the episode's first act and exploits the show's core form brilliantly.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: where are you going with this, Bret?

jokes.text: Jemaine interjects mid-song: 'Where are you going with this, Bret?'
jokes.setup: Bret's song has been going for over half a minute with increasingly florid imagery
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's exasperated but calm interruption treats the song as if it's a rambling anecdote that needs to get to the point
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan straight-man beat. The joke is that Jemaine treats a sweeping musical number as equivalent to a long-winded story that isn't going anywhere. His patience isn't infinite — it just extends far enough to make his complaint funnier when it arrives. Economy score is high because five words do all the work.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: when was this?

jokes.text: Jemaine interrupts the song again: 'Bret, could you please move over to your right?' — he's trying to watch TV
jokes.setup: The song has escalated to moonlit boulevards and silhouettes in mist
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's concern is not the absurdity of a musical confession but that Bret is blocking the television
jokes.explanation: One of the best beats in the episode. The layered bathos — Bret performing an emotionally sincere song, Jemaine concerned only with sightlines to the TV — is a perfect expression of the show's comic worldview. The specificity ('move over to your right') is what makes it sing. Jemaine doesn't say 'stop singing' or 'shut up'; he just needs a slightly better angle. Justifies 8+ as it's a joke with genuine craft and a perfectly calibrated character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: uh, Bret, could you please move over to your right?

jokes.text: 'I'm trying to watch TV.'
jokes.setup: Bret's song continues with romantic imagery about reminiscing
jokes.punchline: Jemaine confirms the previous joke's premise: he is literally watching TV during this musical confession and considers that the priority
jokes.explanation: A continuation beat that reinforces the previous joke rather than building on it. Still funny because deadpan confirmation of an absurd premise works, but it's a coda rather than an escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: oh, yeah? Sounds a bit gay.

jokes.text: 'Sounds a bit gay.'
jokes.setup: Bret sings about reminiscing about reminiscing — a beautifully recursive romantic image
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's only literary criticism of the entire song is this two-word non-critique, delivered flatly
jokes.explanation: Works primarily through economy and Jemaine's delivery. The joke is that this is his one engaged response to the song and it's reductive. The 'gay' epithet reads as Jemaine's limited vocabulary for 'overly sentimental' rather than a slur, which is consistent with character. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: so did you... did you get the bread, or...

jokes.text: 'So did you... did you get the bread, or...'
jokes.setup: Bret has completed an entire romantic ballad about a mysterious woman
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's first question after the song concludes is the original one: did he get the bread
jokes.explanation: Perfect structural callback. The entire opening song has been an elaborate non-answer to 'where's the bread' and Jemaine's patient return to that original question — with the trailing 'or...' suggesting he already suspects the answer — is excellent joke architecture. The ellipsis is doing enormous work. Justifies 8+ because the callback lands exactly where it should, the structure is airtight, and the character voice is pure Jemaine: impassive, practical, unmoved by the preceding spectacle.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: she said her name was a secret then she said her name was cheri...

jokes.text: Bret: 'Oh. No, I forgot.' / Jemaine: 'Cause I've been waiting here with all my sandwich fillings all ready.'
jokes.setup: We expected Bret to have some excuse or partial redemption; he's been on an elaborate mental adventure
jokes.punchline: The bread is simply forgotten, and Jemaine's injury is revealed: he has arranged all his sandwich fillings and has been sitting with them, waiting
jokes.explanation: The image of Jemaine sitting with his sandwich fillings all ready — the specific word 'ready,' the patient domesticity of it — is genuinely charming comedy. The mundane pathos of this detail grounds the absurdity of the preceding song.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: is her middle name cheri? So it's a secret cheri maybe?

jokes.text: Bret: 'I was just really busy thinking about this girl.' / Jemaine: 'What was her name?' — then Bret continues singing the answer
jokes.setup: Normal conversation has resumed; Jemaine asks a normal question
jokes.punchline: Bret slips back into song to answer, treating the melodic mode as interchangeable with speech
jokes.explanation: The joke is the seamless transition between modes — Bret treats song as a natural conversational register. This is core FOTC form exploited well.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: oh, frigid

jokes.text: Bret sings her name is a secret, then sings her name is Cheri. Jemaine: 'Is her middle name Cheri? So it's a secret Cheri maybe?'
jokes.setup: Bret sings that her name is a secret, then immediately sings her name
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's logical reconciliation — maybe 'secret' is her first name and 'Cheri' is her middle name — is delivered as genuine problem-solving
jokes.explanation: Lovely bit of logical comedy. Jemaine treats the lyrical contradiction as a genuine puzzle to be solved rather than poetic license, which is exactly right for his character. 'Secret Cheri' is a funny proper-noun coinage delivered with complete sincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bret, did any of this actually happen?

jokes.text: Jemaine's running commentary on Bret's romantic descriptions: 'dirty?', 'that's rare', 'oh nice', 'oh, frigid', 'quite old', 'oh, 13?'
jokes.setup: Bret sings poetic descriptions of the girl — 'like a Parisian river,' 'like a chocolate eclair,' 'eyes were reflections of eyes,' 'like a winter's morning,' 'comparable to Cleopatra,' 'like Shakespeare's Juliet'
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's flat interjections each take the metaphor at its most literal, unflattering face value — Parisian river = dirty; winter's morning = frigid; Cleopatra = quite old; Juliet = 13
jokes.explanation: The single best sustained comedic sequence in the episode and one of Flight of the Conchords' most technically impressive running gags. Each of Jemaine's interjections follows the same logical rule (take romantic metaphor literally, extract the least flattering implication), but the escalation is beautifully managed: 'dirty' and 'rare' are mild, 'frigid' lands harder, and 'quite old' / 'oh, 13?' are increasingly absurd revelations hiding in canonical romance. 'Oh, 13?' is particularly sharp — the audience has to make the cognitive leap before realizing he's right that Juliet is 13. This earns its 8.9 because it's perfectly structured, deeply character-specific (only Jemaine applies this particular brand of literal skepticism), and each installment builds on the last. A signature FotC moment.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Bret reveals 95% was made up after elaborate fantasy

jokes.text: Bret: 'she reminded me of a winter's morning.' Jemaine: 'Oh, frigid.'
jokes.setup: Another romantic simile, this one a standard poetic comparison
jokes.punchline: Jemaine takes 'winter's morning' literally and concludes the woman is frigid — both the temperature meaning and the slang meaning simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Strong double-meaning joke. 'Frigid' works as a literal temperature reading and as a sexual implication about the woman's character. The ambiguity is the joke — Jemaine's face presumably remains impassive regardless of which reading he intends. One of the sharpest beats in this sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I made 95% of that up. Which bit's true? I saw a girl.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Bret, did any of this actually happen?'
jokes.setup: The song has reached its most absurdly poetic point — rivers of ribbon, sailboats of song
jokes.punchline: Jemaine finally voices what the audience has been thinking: a simple, patient, reasonable question
jokes.explanation: Good structural beat. Jemaine's patience has run its course. The comedy is in how long he waited to ask the obvious question.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: So your excuse for not getting the bread is because you were on a fantasy date?

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Why don't you go on a real date?' Bret: 'Because they're never as good as fantasy dates.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine pushes Bret toward real-world action
jokes.punchline: Bret's reasoning is completely sound — fantasy dates are objectively better — delivered with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational joke with genuine character truth. 'They're never as good as fantasy dates' is funny because it's completely correct and completely pathetic simultaneously. Strong quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Why don't you go on a real date?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Because they're never as good as fantasy dates.

jokes.text: Bret: 'She works down at the cheap zoo.' Jemaine: 'The pet store?'
jokes.setup: Bret describes the girl's workplace
jokes.punchline: 'Cheap zoo' is Bret's sincere descriptor for a pet store — Jemaine translates it into normal English
jokes.explanation: 'Cheap zoo' is an inspired coinage. It's not wrong — a pet store is structurally similar to a zoo but cheaper and more accessible. The joke is that Bret means it sincerely and Jemaine's correction ('the pet store?') confirms the reader's instinct while also revealing that Bret's vocabulary is slightly off-register with the world. Highly quotable, completely original as a descriptor. Justifies 8+ — this is the kind of throwaway that becomes a fan-favorite quote.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: She works down at the cheap zoo.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: The pet store?

jokes.text: Bret: 'She's, um, one of the cat tamers.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine confirms it's a pet store; Bret describes her job
jokes.punchline: 'Cat tamer' — a magnificently inflated job title for a pet store employee who presumably manages cats
jokes.explanation: A companion joke to 'cheap zoo' — Bret's world-building inflates mundane pet store work into exotic animal handling. 'Cat tamer' implies domesticated cats are dangerous enough to require taming, which is its own absurdist premise. The consistency of Bret's slightly-off vocabulary makes both jokes better together.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She's, um, one of the cat tamers.

jokes.text: Every time Bret goes in to see her he freezes up and ends up buying two goldfish he doesn't even want
jokes.setup: Bret describes his difficulty approaching the girl at the pet store
jokes.punchline: His anxiety manifests as involuntary goldfish purchases — a specific, absurd detail
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. Social anxiety manifesting as compulsive goldfish purchase is a perfect absurdist specific detail. Sets up the running goldfish gag for the episode.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Why do you buy two then?' Bret: 'Well, if you get two, you get one free.' Jemaine: 'But you don't even want any goldfish, Bret. Why would you buy two?' Bret: 'Might as well get one free.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine points out the irrationality of buying goldfish you don't want
jokes.punchline: Bret's coupon logic — 'might as well get one free' — applies deal-seeking behavior to purchases he never wanted, compounding the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Strong escalating logic joke. The exchange builds: Jemaine points out the irrationality, Bret explains the deal logic, Jemaine points out the deal logic is still irrational, Bret doubles down. 'Might as well get one free' is the punchline — perfectly circular reasoning that is both correct and completely wrong simultaneously. Justifies 8+ as a structure-driven joke with genuine comedic craft.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, if you get two, you get one free. But you don't even want any goldfish, Bret. Why would you buy two? Might as well get one free.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Oh! No.' / 'Do I have to?' / 'Okay. All right.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks Jemaine to be his wingman 'like in Top Gun'
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's reaction arc — immediate refusal, child-like complaint, resigned acceptance — all in a few seconds
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy. Jemaine's protest/capitulation cycle is a running character dynamic. Nothing exceptional here but it's well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh! When?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Always.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh, okay.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: So you do something every time, and suddenly you've got a reputation.

jokes.text: Bret: 'You can't hit on her.' Jemaine: 'What? Why... why would I hit on her?' Bret: 'You always do that.' Jemaine: 'Oh! When?' Bret: 'Always.' Jemaine: 'Oh, okay. So you do something every time, and suddenly you've got a reputation.'
jokes.setup: Bret lays down the condition that Jemaine not hit on his crush
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's denial has a telling defensive shape — 'so you do something every time and suddenly you've got a reputation' — which is an admission dressed as a grievance
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific joke. Jemaine's complaint about getting a reputation for doing something 'every time' is a self-owning defense — the logic only works if he does it repeatedly. Quotable as a general-purpose excuse.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What are you doing in here?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Fixing the shower.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It wasn't broken.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sorry... I broke it.

jokes.text: Bret: 'What are you doing in here?' Dave: 'Fixing the shower.' Bret: 'It wasn't broken.' Dave: 'Well, I'm sorry... I broke it.'
jokes.setup: Dave is found in their bathroom uninvited
jokes.punchline: Dave broke the shower in order to have a reason to fix it — delivered as if this is a completely reasonable explanation
jokes.explanation: Classic Dave absurdist logic. He created the problem he's solving. The pause before 'I broke it' is the key beat — the slight apology for what he clearly did intentionally.

dialogue.line: Murray breaking then 'fixing' the shower

jokes.text: At the pet store, Bret asks Jemaine to take a photo of him in front of the 'budgebears' (budgerigars)
jokes.setup: They've arrived at the pet store to execute the plan
jokes.punchline: Bret's priority is a tourist-style photo in front of the budgies, delaying the actual mission; plus 'budgebears' is a Bret-specific mispronunciation
jokes.explanation: Gentle stalling-as-character-comedy bit. 'Budgebears' is a funny Bret malapropisim. The photo request reveals Bret's avoidance behavior and his slightly-off linguistic register.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Hey! What's up, guys?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Hey, Dave. What's with the fish, Bret?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I've got a girlfriend.

jokes.text: Bret approaches Savannah: 'Hello.' / 'Are you thirsty?' / [panics] 'Goldfish... one goldfish, please.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has pushed Bret to ask her out; he approaches
jokes.punchline: He starts with 'are you thirsty?' — already off — then abandons the attempt entirely and defaults to buying a goldfish, exactly as established
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff. The goldfish-buying compulsion, established two minutes ago, executes exactly on cue. 'Are you thirsty?' as an opener is its own cringe joke — neither a compliment nor a question that makes sense in context — and then the retreat to the goldfish is the punchline. 'One goldfish, please' as social escape hatch is perfect. Justifies 8+ as a structurally satisfying callback with strong cringe comedy.

dialogue.line: Dave's confusion about their existence/New Zealand

jokes.text: Savannah: 'Um, if you buy two, you...' [trailing off, implying the deal]
jokes.setup: Bret has specified 'one goldfish'
jokes.punchline: Savannah begins to offer the two-for-one deal — the exact trap that has gotten Bret into this mess — implying he's about to buy two goldfish he doesn't want again
jokes.explanation: Elegant callback. The trailing-off punchline implies Bret is about to be ensnared by the deal again. The joke is entirely in the structure — no new information needed, just the setup paying off. Savannah inadvertently perpetuating the cycle is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Guys, women like three things: Men in kilts, southern comfort, and Chris isaak's 'wicked game.'
Whoa, he does know more than you.

jokes.text: Bret buys two goldfish again, invoking the buy-two-get-one-free logic mid-panic
jokes.setup: The buy-two-get-one-free logic established earlier; Savannah starts to explain the deal
jokes.punchline: Bret has preemptively bought two again, mid-nervous breakdown, before she can even finish the sentence
jokes.explanation: The callback lands because the transcript implies Savannah starts the 'if you buy two...' pitch and Bret just says 'okay' — meaning he's purchased two goldfish he doesn't want yet again. The running total of unwanted goldfish is the joke.

dialogue.line: Murray's elaborate band meeting protocol for three people

jokes.text: Bret's second conversational attempt: 'What time do you feed the cats?' — followed by silence and scene cut
jokes.setup: Bret is trying to extend conversation with Savannah after buying goldfish
jokes.punchline: His best conversational follow-up is logistical information about cat feeding schedules, followed by a scene cut implying total failure
jokes.explanation: The twelve-second gap between this line and the next scene marker [05:39 to ~05:45] is the joke. Whatever Savannah's reaction was, it clearly ended the interaction. 'What time do you feed the cats?' is the most awkward possible attempt to prolong contact — specific, utilitarian, vaguely creepy given his interest in her.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: uh, to be honest with you, at the end of the day, um... actually, I think greg could be responsible.

jokes.text: Bret: 'Not... not completely a girlfriend.' / 'She doesn't know I exist.'
jokes.setup: Bret is walking back his 'girlfriend' claim
jokes.punchline: 'Not completely a girlfriend' is a magnificent understatement for someone who is emphatically not a girlfriend at all; compounded by 'she doesn't know I exist'
jokes.explanation: 'Not completely a girlfriend' is an excellent phrase — the qualifier 'completely' does a lot of work, implying there's a spectrum of girlfriend-ness on which this situation falls somewhere. Combined with 'she doesn't know I exist,' it's a self-undermining cascade.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, it's like... it's kind of like a habit. You know, we sort of fall into these patterns. Day after day, we end up doing the same sort of thing.

jokes.text: Dave: 'But you do... you do exist, don't you?' — then the reveal that the boys previously claimed not to exist / to be from Never Never Land
jokes.setup: Dave takes Bret's 'she doesn't know I exist' literally
jokes.punchline: Dave's concern is legitimate based on prior conversations in which Bret and Jemaine apparently told him they were from Never Never Land and had 'flown' there
jokes.explanation: Rich worldbuilding joke. Dave's reality is one where Bret and Jemaine may have previously convinced him they were non-corporeal beings from Never Never Land. 'We flew here.' 'On a plane. On a plane, Dave.' The escalating clarifications are excellent. Dave's sincerity is the key — he's not stupid, he's just operating on different information.

dialogue.line: Murray's relationship metaphor about old sneakers

jokes.text: Bret and Jemaine insisting 'I fully exist' with increasing emphasis
jokes.setup: Dave has questioned their existence
jokes.punchline: 'I fully exist' — the adverb 'fully' suggests there are degrees of existence and Bret is at maximum
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist follow-through. 'Fully exist' is a funny qualifier.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: 'Chicken torso' as Bret's codename

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'I know about brassieres. Do you know about those?' — in response to Dave questioning his knowledge of women
jokes.setup: Dave says 'What the fuck does he know about women?'
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's single qualifier for his expertise in women is that he knows what a bra is — stated with complete confidence as if this is adequate
jokes.explanation: Excellent. The joke is that 'I know about brassieres' is simultaneously the most and least helpful credential for understanding women. The formal word 'brassieres' rather than 'bras' is part of the joke — Jemaine's slightly formal register making him sound both more and less worldly. Justifies 8+ as a highly quotable, character-specific line with perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Stand with your feet apart like your balls are on fire. Don't do the accompanying face, though... just the stance.

jokes.text: 'Whoa, he does know more than you.'
jokes.setup: Dave lists his three-item theory of what women want
jokes.punchline: Bret immediately concedes that Dave has superior knowledge to Jemaine — delivered completely straight
jokes.explanation: A strong reaction beat that validates Dave's absurd credentials while gently roasting Jemaine. Works through economy and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Half ignore her and half pay attention to her.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Hello.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Turn side on.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Say somethin you assho!

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Compliment her hair.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Ask her what's up with her face.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What's up with your face?

jokes.text: Dave's theory on women: 'Men in kilts, southern comfort, and Chris Isaak's Wicked Game.'
jokes.setup: Dave positions himself as the women expert
jokes.punchline: His three-part theory is a bizarre but internally consistent list — the specificity of 'Wicked Game' in particular is the comedic anchor
jokes.explanation: Dave's specific and confident nonsense is his comedic brand. The list works because it's oddly credible: these three things do have a certain romantic-trashy atmosphere in common. The specificity of 'Chris Isaak's Wicked Game' elevates it from generic absurdism to something with texture.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Tell her you're freaky.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Why would you tell her that he's freaky?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: How's he gonna get into her pants if he doesn't...

jokes.text: Murray's band meeting: present / present / present. 'That wasn't too hard, was it?'
jokes.setup: Murray interrupts mid-conversation to call a formal band meeting with Greg present
jokes.punchline: The meeting consists entirely of taking attendance and Murray congratulating everyone on completing the attendance
jokes.explanation: Pure Murray comedy. His managerial bureaucracy — calling a meeting to take attendance, congratulating the room on successfully attending — is consistent character work. 'That wasn't too hard, was it?' is the exact wrong thing to say to people who didn't know why they were called in.

dialogue.line: Mel's stalking advice including collecting nail clippings

jokes.text: Murray's elaborate phone call to Helen: blaming Greg for eating the four missing sandwiches using fabricated forensic evidence — 'crumbs just around his mouth'
jokes.setup: Four sandwiches are missing from a meeting with the Chinese ambassador; Murray is on the phone with Helen
jokes.punchline: Murray constructs an elaborate, specific case against Greg — wrappers in his bin, crumbs at the corner of his mouth — prefaced with 'I'm not pointing any fingers' while pointing every finger
jokes.explanation: Excellent sustained Murray joke. The escalation is the comedy: wrappers in the bin, crumbs on mouth, 'I'm not pointing any fingers' followed by specific finger-pointing, 'the third suspect' (implying first and second suspects exist). Murray's bureaucratic cowardice rendered as a quasi-forensic investigation is perfect character work. The 'crumbs just on each side' specificity is what earns the high score.

dialogue.line: Going through Savannah's garbage and knowing specific bill amounts

jokes.text: Greg heard the entire conversation — he was standing right there
jokes.setup: Murray has just finished an elaborate phone blame-session directed at Greg
jokes.punchline: Murray and Jemaine acknowledge Greg almost certainly heard everything; Murray apparently conducted the accusation while Greg stood beside him
jokes.explanation: Good cringe payoff to the sandwich phone call. Murray's obliviousness extends to not registering that his accusation target was present for the entire accusation.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: when you love someone, you need all the ammunition you can get to take them down.

jokes.text: Murray's schedule book says 'Nothing' / 'No need to come in.' — he called them in to tell them there's no need to come in
jokes.setup: Bret asks if there are any gigs; Murray checks his schedule
jokes.punchline: He literally wrote 'nothing' and 'no need to come in' — and then called them in. He acknowledges this is weird.
jokes.explanation: Classic Murray. The joke is that he's a manager who manages nothing — his organizational instinct (calling meetings, keeping schedules) runs completely independent of whether there's anything to organize. He even wrote 'no need to come in' as a scheduled entry. The fact that he admits 'that's weird, isn't it?' shows minimal self-awareness arriving too late.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Business stuff... you know, stock exchange, calculators.

jokes.text: Murray's digression about Shelley: 'It's a bit like shelley and I, actually, looking back... just getting used to each other so much, taking each other for granted like a pair of old sneakers.'
jokes.setup: Murray is explaining why he calls pointless band meetings — 'it's kind of like a habit'
jokes.punchline: The explanation unexpectedly becomes a melancholy meditation on his failed marriage, delivered to two musicians who wanted to know about gigs
jokes.explanation: Murray's sadness sneaking into band meetings is a recurring character texture that works well here. 'Like a pair of old sneakers' is a specific enough simile to feel authentic. The tonal shift is gentle — not played for big laughs, more for warmth and discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: I told you... did I not explain that? You know, this is bullshit. I didn't come out here to do pro Bono work.

jokes.text: Murray asks Greg for orange cake — Greg responds with 'It's Murray' instead of yes or no, demonstrating ongoing anger
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to smooth things over; asks Greg casually if there's cake left
jokes.punchline: Greg's response — 'It's Murray' — is not an answer to the question but an announcement to whoever else is there, refusing to directly interact with Murray
jokes.explanation: Good callback payoff. Greg's passive-aggressive non-response — treating Murray as if he's a named visitor to be announced rather than the person addressing him — is a creative expression of anger. 'It's Murray' is a funny specific choice.

dialogue.line: The mugging going wrong with John actually stealing the purse

jokes.text: Jemaine in the van on walkie-talkie: 'Chicken torso. Chicken torso, can you hear me?' — Bret's call sign
jokes.setup: Jemaine is in a van with a walkie-talkie earpiece, coaching Bret at the pet store
jokes.punchline: 'Chicken torso' as Bret's code name — why chicken torso? The joke is that no explanation is given or needed
jokes.explanation: 'Chicken torso' as a code name is an inspired piece of absurdist naming. It's not cute (kitten), not tough (eagle), not functional — it's just a body part of a poultry animal, delivered with complete procedural seriousness. Jemaine repeating it as a radio call adds to the comedy. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+ for pure originality and unexplainable specificity that defines absurdist comedy at its best.

jokes.text: Jemaine's wingman callsign: 'Chicken torso'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is set up in a van with a radio to advise Bret in the pet store
jokes.punchline: He addresses Bret as 'Chicken torso' — a callsign that has no apparent logic or military context
jokes.explanation: 'Chicken torso' is a perfect non-sequitur callsign. Its comedy comes entirely from its specificity — not 'Eagle' or 'Ghost' but 'Chicken torso.' The seriousness with which Jemaine uses it ('Chicken torso, raise a hand if you can hear me') elevates it further. Highly quotable, strongly absurdist.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: girl, I'm gonna take the month of august off just to get you off

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Stand with your feet apart like your balls are on fire. Don't do the accompanying face, though... just the stance.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is coaching Bret's body language via earpiece
jokes.punchline: The stance advice is surreally specific and anatomically unpleasant; the follow-up — 'don't do the accompanying face' — acknowledges that he's aware the image creates an involuntary expression
jokes.explanation: One of the standout jokes of the episode. The 'balls on fire' stance is absurdly specific as a confidence posture instruction. But the amendment — 'don't do the accompanying face' — is the real genius: it demonstrates Jemaine has thought through the full embodied reality of this stance and is trying to isolate only the useful parts. The joke is perfectly constructed: setup (stance instruction) → complications (the face) → correction. Justifies 8+ comfortably: comparable to the 8.0 anchor in its precision and surprise.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm freaky

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'No no, stay facing away from her. It makes you seem different.' / 'Half ignore her and half pay attention to her.'
jokes.setup: Bret has turned toward Savannah; Jemaine gives further coaching
jokes.punchline: 'Half ignore her and half pay attention to her' — a completely meaningless instruction that Jemaine delivers as expert advice; the justification 'it makes you seem different' is equally useless
jokes.explanation: 'Makes you seem different' as a pickup rationale is a funny non-explanation. 'Half ignore, half pay attention' is the comedic soul of bad pickup artist advice rendered in the most literal possible way.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: let's take my body and we'll cover it with honey

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: stick some money to the honey

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: now I'm covered in money, honey

jokes.text: Simultaneous conflicting advice: Dave tells Bret to ask about her face, Jemaine tells him to compliment her hair
jokes.setup: Bret has two advisors in his ear giving real-time coaching
jokes.punchline: Jemaine says 'compliment her hair,' Dave says 'ask her what's up with her face' — Bret naturally follows Dave's advice
jokes.explanation: The competing advice structure is well-exploited here. 'Ask her what's up with her face' is genuinely aggressive/confusing advice that Dave delivers with the confidence of someone who just explained the three things women want. The fact that Bret follows Dave's advice over Jemaine's is a perfect character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Savannah
dialogue.line: How do you know about my electricity Bill?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, I just went through your rubbish and I found your Bill.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Compliment her hair.' Dave: 'Ask her what's up with her face.' Bret follows Dave's advice: 'What's up with your face?'
jokes.setup: Two voices giving conflicting advice; Bret has to choose
jokes.punchline: Bret inexplicably follows Dave's wildly worse advice and asks Savannah 'What's up with your face?'
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe escalation. The joke has three layers: Dave gives objectively terrible advice, Jemaine gives reasonable advice, Bret follows Dave's. The fact that Bret defaults to the worst option is consistent with his anxious/confused character. 'What's up with your face?' as a pickup line is absurdly horrifying.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She's a bit crazy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Do you want me to give you a hand breaking up with her?

jokes.text: 'You shouldn't have said that. It's too early.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks Savannah 'what's up with your face?' and she reacts with confusion
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's diagnosis: the problem isn't the question itself, just the timing — 'too early'
jokes.explanation: Sharp. The joke is that Jemaine's objection to 'what's up with your face?' is purely temporal — he implies this is eventually an appropriate question, just not now. This reframes a disqualifying insult as a conversation phase issue. Clean, economical, character-true.

jokes.text: Savannah: 'Is that a walkie-talkie on your kilt?' Jemaine: 'Tell her it's an iPod.' Bret: 'It's an old iPod.' Savannah: 'Okay.'
jokes.setup: Savannah has noticed the walkie-talkie; Jemaine coaches a cover story
jokes.punchline: 'An old iPod' — a completely implausible lie that Savannah accepts with 'okay,' which is its own joke about either her credulity or her desire to end the interaction
jokes.explanation: Savannah's 'okay' is a small masterpiece of ambiguity — is she buying it? Does she not care? Both options are funny. The 'old iPod' specificity adds texture.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Now tell her you're freaky.' Dave: 'Don't tell her that.' / Dave: 'Tell her you're a freak.' / Jemaine: 'Why would you tell her that he's freaky?' Dave (off-mic): 'How's he gonna get into her pants if he doesn't...'
jokes.setup: The two coaches disagree on the next move
jokes.punchline: Dave and Jemaine accidentally swap positions — Jemaine says freaky, Dave says don't, then Dave says freak, and Dave's justification involves a crudeness that Jemaine finds unreasonable — all while Bret stands there
jokes.explanation: Functional confused-coaches escalation. Sets up the 'I'm Freaky' song but as a standalone beat it's middling.

jokes.text: Savannah: 'Can I help you with something?' Bret: 'I'll just... I'll just get a goldfish, please.'
jokes.setup: The entire coaching sequence has collapsed; Savannah addresses Bret directly
jokes.punchline: Bret retreats to goldfish again — third goldfish purchase, fully establishing the running gag
jokes.explanation: Third iteration of the goldfish callback, now fully a running gag. Each iteration reinforces Bret's pattern and deepens the absurdity. By this point, the audience is ahead of the joke which is why it works.

jokes.text: Mel accuses Jemaine and Dave of watching her for three and a half hours — then becomes disappointed when told it's not about her
jokes.setup: Mel discovers the van; assumes she's the target of surveillance
jokes.punchline: Mel's initial outrage ('that is so creepy') flips when she learns it's not about her — she's briefly disappointed that she's not the one being stalked
jokes.explanation: Excellent Mel joke. Her character is defined by her obsessive fixation on Bret and Jemaine, so her reaction arc — outrage → disappointment — is perfectly calibrated. The best Mel moments exploit the gap between her presented outrage and her actual desire to be stalked.

jokes.text: Mel has apparently been watching Bret and Jemaine from outside for three and a half hours — but assumed they were watching HER
jokes.setup: Mel approaches Bret and Jemaine standing outside the pet store, apparently for hours
jokes.punchline: Her immediate assumption is that the surveillance operation is focused on her — and her subsequent reaction ('That is so creepy') applies perfectly to herself
jokes.explanation: Mel's introduction is a masterclass in character economy. Her first instinct — 'is it me?' — immediately establishes her as someone who assumes all surveillance is aimed at her. Her accusation 'that is an invasion of my personal space' while not realizing she's about to go through someone's rubbish is the ironic setup for everything that follows. Character integration is nearly perfect — this is Mel in three lines.

jokes.text: Mel on finding the key to someone's heart: 'I like to find useful information in bills, letters. Sometimes I collect nail clippings, but that's just me.'
jokes.setup: Mel is giving Bret advice about getting to know someone you love
jokes.punchline: 'Sometimes I collect nail clippings' — delivered as a minor personal quirk ('but that's just me') rather than a deeply disturbing behavior
jokes.explanation: Strong dark-subversive Mel joke. The escalation from 'reading bills' (concerning) to 'collecting nail clippings' (actively disturbing) is perfectly paced, and 'but that's just me' as the dismissive qualifier transforms a stalker behavior into a casual personality trait. Justifies 8+ for the perfect character integration — only Mel could deliver this.

jokes.text: Mel: 'Bret, does she run an Indian food takeaway?' — then produces Savannah's rubbish
jokes.setup: Mel is suggesting ways to get to know Savannah
jokes.punchline: Mel has already gone through Savannah's actual rubbish and has specific intelligence about her: she loves eggs, her name is Savannah, she works at Petland, she has $145 on her electricity bill
jokes.explanation: Good sustained Mel stalker comedy. The specificity of '$145 electricity bill' as intelligence gathered from someone's rubbish is the comedic high point. Mel has done extensive advance work on a stranger's behalf.

jokes.text: Mel: 'When you love someone, you need all the ammunition you can get to take them down.'
jokes.setup: Mel is synthesizing her romantic philosophy
jokes.punchline: The military/warfare metaphor for love — 'ammunition,' 'take them down' — reveals Mel's fundamentally adversarial conception of romance
jokes.explanation: One of the best Mel lines in the episode. 'All the ammunition you can get to take them down' expresses her romantic worldview perfectly — love as siege warfare, the beloved as enemy combatant. The casual delivery makes it funnier. Justifies 8+ as a highly quotable, deeply character-specific line.

jokes.text: Mel's account of how she and Doug got together: 'He resisted at first. In the end he cracked.'
jokes.setup: Mel is citing her own relationship as proof that her tactics work
jokes.punchline: 'He resisted at first. In the end he cracked.' — framed as a love story, structured as a hostage negotiation
jokes.explanation: Compact dark-subversive joke. 'He resisted... he cracked' is a perfect two-beat structure that reframes romantic pursuit as psychological warfare. Economy is very high — eight words convey an entire disturbing relationship history.

jokes.text: Mel: 'Mel, this dumpster is definitely from a different building.' / Mel: 'Keep looking, Doug.'
jokes.setup: The gang is searching Savannah's rubbish for intelligence
jokes.punchline: Doug (Mel's husband) is also digging through dumpsters on Mel's orders — the domestic detail that he's been deployed to go through rubbish while she coaches others is quietly disturbing
jokes.explanation: Good ambient dark comedy. Doug's presence as Mel's dedicated rubbish-operative extends her character's stalker world into the domestic partnership. 'Keep looking, Doug' is a good command-voice gag.

jokes.text: Mel: 'I think she has man's feet.' / 'Man feet, Bret.'
jokes.setup: Mel is sharing intelligence from the rubbish
jokes.punchline: The intelligence is: man feet. Delivered with concern and repeated for emphasis.
jokes.explanation: Funny because of the specificity and the two-part delivery ('Man feet, Bret' as clarifying emphasis). The detective work has produced a warning about feet morphology.

jokes.text: Doug: 'Ah. Motorcycle magazines.' Mel: 'She must like tough guys.' Bret and Jemaine: 'Tough guys?'
jokes.setup: Intelligence-gathering from rubbish continues
jokes.punchline: Motorcycle magazines lead to 'she likes tough guys' — this is clearly information that does not flatter Bret or Jemaine, who are immediately aware of their non-toughness
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat. Sets up the mugging plan's motivation. The boys' reaction ('Tough guys?') is the right response but doesn't add much.

jokes.text: Murray wants Jemaine as wingman to apologize to Greg — instructs Jemaine to discuss 'business stuff... stock exchange, calculators'
jokes.setup: Murray asks Jemaine to distract Greg's companion while he apologizes
jokes.punchline: Murray's conception of business conversation is 'stock exchange, calculators' — a list of financial words with no connecting logic
jokes.explanation: Good Murray joke. 'Calculators' as the second item in a business-conversation list is funny — it's technically business-related equipment but not a topic of conversation. Murray's limited conception of professional life on display.

jokes.text: Murray's strategy for distracting Greg's businessman contact: 'Business stuff — you know, stock exchange, calculators.'
jokes.setup: Murray asks Jemaine to distract Greg's colleague while he apologizes
jokes.punchline: His entire concept of business conversation is 'stock exchange, calculators' — the most generic possible surface-level understanding
jokes.explanation: A compact, character-perfect joke. Murray has reduced all of business to two nouns. 'Calculators' as a conversational topic with a businessman is especially funny because it's an object rather than a concept — Murray apparently thinks businessmen enjoy talking about their tools.

jokes.text: Murray's apology to Greg: 'I like your tie.' — as an opener
jokes.setup: Murray is working up to a genuine apology; he opens with a compliment
jokes.punchline: 'I like your tie' — an awkward opener that tries to butter Greg up before the apology, delivered with visible calculation
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe beat. 'I like your tie' as an apology opener is a recognizable social awkwardness move. Gets the job done.

jokes.text: Murray's squashed chocolates from his desk: 'They're a bit squashed, but...'
jokes.setup: Murray is attempting to make amends with gifts
jokes.punchline: The chocolates are desk-squashed — clearly not purchased specifically for Greg, just emergency desk candy pulled out at the last moment
jokes.explanation: Nice small character detail. The squashed chocolates indicate the apology was improvised and the gesture is perfunctory. Murray's managerial affect trying to paper over real damage with inadequate gestures.

jokes.text: Murray: 'No breaking through that wall of ice, is there?' — to Jemaine, while Greg silently takes another chocolate
jokes.setup: Greg has accepted the chocolates but hasn't forgiven Murray
jokes.punchline: Murray identifies the emotional stalemate — 'wall of ice' — while Greg in fact just accepted another chocolate, suggesting some thawing
jokes.explanation: The visual contradiction — Murray laments the wall of ice while Greg eats his chocolates — is the joke. Functional.

jokes.text: Jemaine, meant to be distracting the businessman Sven, has gotten deeply into a game: '2-3-6... this is a fun game. Do you ever play it online?'
jokes.setup: Jemaine was supposed to distract Greg's companion as a wingman favor
jokes.punchline: He's become genuinely engaged in some unspecified game with Sven and doesn't want to leave
jokes.explanation: Good reversal — the distraction worked too well and now Jemaine must be extracted. The numbers '2-3-6' as in-progress game notation with no context is a funny detail.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Bret, I don't want to be your wingman anymore.' Bret: 'What? Why not?' Jemaine: 'It's tiring. I'm exhausted.'
jokes.setup: After the pet store debacle and the business distraction
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's reason for quitting: he's tired. Not humiliated, not frustrated with the plan — just physically tired.
jokes.explanation: Good Jemaine character moment. His objection is scaled-down and mundane — not moral or emotional, just fatigue. Consistent with the show's deflation of drama into the pedestrian.

jokes.text: Bret's plan: 'I bump into Savannah tonight, you mug us and I beat you up a little bit.' / 'I saw it on a sitcom.' / 'Did it work on the sitcom?' / 'Not completely, no, but this is real, so I think we've got a better chance.'
jokes.setup: Bret proposes the mugging plan
jokes.punchline: His confidence in the plan vs. the sitcom is based on the premise that reality beats fiction — despite the plan coming directly from fiction and having already failed in fiction
jokes.explanation: One of the strongest meta-comedy beats in the episode. The joke is in the logic: it didn't work on the sitcom, but we're in reality so it'll work better — except we're watching a sitcom. 'Not completely, no' as the qualifier for the sitcom's failure is perfectly chosen — not a total failure, just 'not completely' successful. Justifies 8+ for the meta-comedy layer and the airtight logical structure that falls apart on examination.

jokes.text: Savannah recognizes Bret: 'the goldfish guy... like, can I get a goldfish?'
jokes.setup: Bret attempts a casual encounter on the street
jokes.punchline: Savannah's mental tag for Bret is 'the goldfish guy' — his entire identity to her is compulsive goldfish purchasing, confirmed by her mimicry
jokes.explanation: Good callback payoff. The goldfish purchases have not gone unnoticed — Bret is known exclusively by his nervous compulsion. The running gag reaches its proper conclusion here.

jokes.text: Bret: 'Yeah, I've bought, like, 63 goldfish off you.'
jokes.setup: Savannah recognizes him as the goldfish guy
jokes.punchline: 63 goldfish. The episode established an anxiety-purchase pattern; Bret quantifies it as 63 goldfish
jokes.explanation: Excellent number joke. '63 goldfish' is the payoff to the running gag and the specific number — not round, not small — implies a long history of anxious purchases. Two goldfish at a time (per the deal), meaning ~32 visits, all ending in goldfish. The specificity of 63 is what makes it so funny versus a round number. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Bret: 'I'm happy to be your bodyguard. This is a dangerous corner.'
jokes.setup: Bret offers to walk Savannah home; sets up the mugging
jokes.punchline: He reassures her this is a dangerous corner as justification — presumably manufacturing a threat
jokes.explanation: Setup beat for the mugging sequence. Functional.

jokes.text: John: 'I didn't come out here to do pro bono work. I do this for a living. This is my profession. You can't take advantage of our friendship like this.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine explains John can't keep the purse
jokes.punchline: John invokes professional ethics, labor rights, and friendship — the full vocabulary of legitimate workplace grievance applied to mugging
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. 'Pro bono work' applied to mugging. The escalation from financial objection to professional dignity to friendship exploitation creates a complete arc. This is absurdism at its best — taking a premise (mugger as professional) to its logical conclusion with complete sincerity. Justifies 8+ firmly.

jokes.text: John in disguise: 'What the fuck is on your face?' / Jemaine: 'I just thought it would make me look more criminal.' / 'Take it off. You're like the Hamburglar. That guy fucking scares me.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has applied some face modification for the mugging
jokes.punchline: John — a professional mugger — is terrified of the Hamburglar reference; his specific fear of the Hamburglar is the joke's unexpected turn
jokes.explanation: Good character joke for John. A professional criminal who is frightened by the Hamburglar reveals unexpected depth. 'That guy fucking scares me' is earnestly delivered, which is the key.

jokes.text: John's appearance: Jemaine says 'What the fuck is on your face?' / 'I just thought it would make me look more criminal.' / 'Take it off. You're like the Hamburglar. That guy fucking scares me.'
jokes.setup: John has prepared for the mugging with some kind of face disguise.
jokes.punchline: He looks like the McDonald's Hamburglar. Jemaine's reaction: not that it's bad criminal disguise, but that the Hamburglar personally frightens him.
jokes.explanation: 'That guy fucking scares me' about the Hamburglar is unexpected and personal — Jemaine's genuine Hamburglar phobia is an absurd non-sequitur that lands well. The visual of whatever John has on his face being worse than expected as a criminal disguise is good physical comedy.

jokes.text: The mugging goes wrong: John forgets to let them keep the purse and actually mugs Savannah
jokes.setup: The staged mugging was supposed to be fake
jokes.punchline: John's professional instincts override the plan — he mugs for real, steals the purse, busts Jemaine's nose
jokes.explanation: Good escalation payoff. John's professional reflexes take over; the plan collapses in the most predictable-yet-funny way. The chaos of the scene — multiple overlapping reactions — works as physical comedy.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'What did you bring him for?' Bret: 'He's the only mugger I know, isn't he?'
jokes.setup: John has caused chaos by actually mugging them
jokes.punchline: Bret's defense for hiring John — 'he's the only mugger I know' — implies this is a reasonable consideration and that knowing even one mugger is a normal occurrence
jokes.explanation: Good economy joke. The implied social network here — Bret has exactly one mugger in his contacts — is funny. The resigned shrug of 'isn't he' adds to it.

jokes.text: Bret to Savannah after the chaos: 'They're not supposed to do that.' / 'Would you still like to get a little bit of sushi?'
jokes.setup: Savannah has just been actually mugged; Bret is attempting damage control
jokes.punchline: His clarification ('they're not supposed to do that') implies oversight of the operation, compounded by immediately pivoting to sushi
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy. Bret's 'they're not supposed to do that' inadvertently confirms he organized the mugging while trying to distance himself from it. The sushi pivot is the perfect Bret move — optimistic, tone-deaf, earnest.

jokes.text: 'Oh, it worked. They're walking away together.' / John: 'I know.' / Jemaine: 'Hey, you weren't maced. Why are you crying?' / John: 'I'm not fucking crying.'
jokes.setup: The plan partially works — Bret and Savannah walk off together
jokes.punchline: John is crying, presumably moved by the success of the romantic scheme he helped (accidentally) execute — but denies it with maximum professionalism
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. John — the professional mugger who complained about pro bono work, who busted Jemaine's nose — is secretly moved by the romance. 'I'm not fucking crying' is a perfect line because the profanity maintains his tough exterior while the content (the denial) undermines it. Jemaine's logical framing ('you weren't maced') is the perfect setup. Justifies 8+ for the character reversal and the economical delivery.

jokes.text: John still has the purse — 'force of habit'
jokes.setup: The mugging is over; Jemaine asks if John has the purse
jokes.punchline: 'It's force of habit' — John stole the purse automatically; professional conditioning overrode the plan
jokes.explanation: Good character callback. 'Force of habit' applied to theft completes John's arc as a professional whose instincts are deeper than his agreements. The brevity is perfect.

jokes.text: Murray uses diplomatic immunity to get Jemaine released — with handmade Kinko's cards: 'Jemaine Clemaine, New Zealand Diplomat'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has been arrested; Murray arrives to negotiate his release
jokes.punchline: Murray presents a Kinko's-printed diplomat card — the officer notes it looks like it was made at Kinko's; Murray confirms and offers to compare badge-getting methods
jokes.explanation: Murray's quasi-diplomacy is a recurring show element and this is a strong instance. 'Jemaine Clemaine' as a spelling on the card suggests it was printed in haste by someone who can't quite remember his own band member's name. 'You can get 1,000 done for $10' as the response to official skepticism is perfectly Murray — explaining the production logistics rather than addressing the forgery.

jokes.text: Murray: 'I'm the deputy cultural attache for the new zealand government, and this is my assistant greg. He's actually angry with me about something, so I've brought him in here as a treat. We don't really need him, but it's good to get him out of the office.'
jokes.setup: Murray is presenting his credentials to the police officer
jokes.punchline: He undermines the professional presentation by noting Greg is there as an emotional pacification treat, and that he's not strictly necessary
jokes.explanation: Excellent Murray moment. He's trying to project authority while simultaneously oversharing Greg's emotional state, his own culpability, and the superfluousness of Greg's presence — to a police officer, mid-crisis. 'It's good to get him out of the office' as a reason for bringing Greg to a police station is perfect Murray logic. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Murray to the officer: 'Can we get my friend John out as well?' Officer: 'No.' Murray: (accepts this instantly and moves on)
jokes.setup: Having successfully extracted Jemaine, Murray tries to also get John released
jokes.punchline: One-word refusal; Murray accepts it without protest and pivots to Greg reconciliation
jokes.explanation: Quick functional beat. Murray's attempt and immediate capitulation is consistent with his character but not remarkable.

jokes.text: Murray and Greg reconcile in the police station: 'I knew bringing you here was a good thing.'
jokes.setup: Murray and Greg have a moment of reconciliation
jokes.punchline: Murray's self-congratulation for bringing Greg to a police station as a relationship-repair strategy — framed as vindication of the plan
jokes.explanation: Good Murray self-satisfaction beat. He's proud of his unconventional but apparently effective management technique.

jokes.text: Officer: 'Gentlemen, I'm actually quite busy at the moment, so if you could maybe do this some other time, maybe?'
jokes.setup: Murray and Greg are reconciling warmly in a police station while the officer waits
jokes.punchline: The officer has to politely suggest they take their emotional moment elsewhere
jokes.explanation: Standard straight-man reaction beat. The doubling of 'maybe' at the end is a nice detail (police officer uncertain of his own suggestion). Functional.

jokes.text: Dave calls via Bret's earpiece at the sushi restaurant: 'Tell her you're freaky, Bret. Tell her you're freaky.'
jokes.setup: Bret is on what appears to be a successful first date
jokes.punchline: Dave is still on the earpiece circuit, coaching, returning to his one piece of advice from hours earlier
jokes.explanation: Good callback that sets up the song. Dave's persistence on 'tell her you're freaky' despite all that's happened is consistent and funny.

jokes.text: Bret: 'I'm freaky.' Savannah: 'What?' Bret: 'Did I mention that I'm one of those sort of freaky guys?' — then the 'I'm Freaky' song begins
jokes.setup: Bret has been advised to tell her he's freaky; it's an absurd piece of advice in a date context
jokes.punchline: Bret takes the advice literally and announces it baldly: 'I'm freaky' — leading into a full musical demonstration
jokes.explanation: Good song-introduction beat. Bret's delivery of 'I'm one of those sort of freaky guys' as if reading from a script given to him is the right character note — confused, earnest, following instructions.

jokes.text: The 'I'm Freaky' song — opening lines: taking August off to get her off, clearing the tabletop requiring a mop
jokes.setup: Bret is demonstrating his freakiness through song
jokes.punchline: The song's opening 'freaky' acts are domestic and logistical — 'take the month of August off,' 'we'll need a mop' — the freakiness requires advance planning and cleaning supplies
jokes.explanation: The 'I'm Freaky' song's comedy hinges on freakiness that requires domestic preparation. 'We'll need a mop' is the first great line — the logistical foresight of sexual freakiness. The song subverts the sexy-confidence genre by being earnest, specific, and mundane.

jokes.text: 'Let's take a photo of a goat in a boat / then we can float in the moat / and be freaky'
jokes.setup: The 'freaky' activities escalate
jokes.punchline: Goat-in-boat photography as a component of freakiness — the rhyme scheme (goat/boat/float/moat) gives it the feel of a children's song applied to adult context
jokes.explanation: One of the song's best lines. The nursery-rhyme sound of 'goat in a boat in a moat' applied to a sexy confidence song is inspired. The activities aren't sexual at all — they're whimsical recreational — which makes it funnier. 'Be freaky' as the conclusion to goat photography is perfectly anticlimactic. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'She's a bit crazy.' / 'Do you want me to give you a hand breaking up with her?'
jokes.setup: Savannah has just kicked Bret out after his confession
jokes.punchline: Jemaine offers to be Bret's wingman for the breakup — another wingman deployment, this time for the opposite purpose
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural close. The wingman concept, introduced as a reluctant favor for getting together, is now offered voluntarily for breaking up. Jemaine's complete reversal — he didn't want to be the wingman at all, now offers proactively — is a perfect character arc button. 'She's a bit crazy' applied to the woman Bret spent the episode trying to impress is also a good ironic note. The symmetry earns a high structure score.

tmdb_overview: Jemaine and Dave help Bret woo the clerk at the cheap zoo (pet shop). Jemaine also helps Murry seek forgiveness from Greg.
